text: The surgeon positions the camera in the anterior seventh intercostal space and places two additional anterior ports for the Articential wristed instruments. The eight-millimeter robotic ports are initially used but later replaced by direct insertion through chest incisions.

The surgeon uses Articential wristed instruments, including the Maryland and Johan dissectors, to manipulate and dissect tissue in the thoracic cavity. The Maryland is a long, thin instrument capable of delivering bipolar energy, while the Johan dissector is similar to its robotic counterpart. An additional 5mm port is placed posteriorly for retraction using a lymph node grasper. The left upper lobe is retracted posteriorly as the surgeon begins dissection of the anterior hilum.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to carefully dissect the pleura over the anterior hilum, exposing the superior pulmonary vein and identifying the pulmonary artery. The wristed instrument allows precise alignment with the tissue, utilizing bipolar energy for dissection.

The surgeon navigates over the top of the hilum, exposing the aortic arch and pulmonary artery. Using wristed instruments, the surgeon achieves a complete ninety-degree angle around the superior pulmonary vein, utilizing the blunt tip of the Johan dissector for confident dissection behind the vein. The Cavidian Tristapler with Signia is then used to secure the area.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments to gently dissect lymph nodes from behind the vein, employing a Maryland dissector for precise manipulation. A tan tri-staple load is fired through the sub-xiphoid port, facilitating the removal of lymph nodes while providing tactile feedback during grasping.

The surgeon uses ArtiCentral wristed instruments to dissect around the anterior segmental pulmonary artery, articulating the Maryland dissector to clean the artery above before proceeding below. The pointed nature of the Maryland allows precise piercing of the remaining tissue, with tactile feedback enhancing control over tissue tension during the dissection.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments to gently dissect around the apical segmental artery, utilizing tactile feedback to assess tissue resistance. The Maryland instrument is employed to pierce the final layer of pleura surrounding the vessel, ensuring precise dissection without resistance.

The surgeon uses Maryland wristed instruments to manipulate smaller vessels, followed by a gold tip for sub-xiphoid access. A complication arises with bleeding from the anterior segmental branch, showing bruising on the artery, possibly due to stapler tugging. Tachycel patches are applied for haemostasis. The surgeon approaches station seven lymph nodes anteriorly, lifting the left main bronchus to access remnant lymph nodes from a previous mediastinoscopy, removing them with non-wristed instruments. The surgeon identifies the lower lobe, noting a large bronchial artery, and searches for the bifurcation of the upper and lower lobe bronchi.

The surgeon uses the Johan wristed instrument to peel off positive N-one lymph nodes containing tumor from the lower lobe bronchus, navigating through sticky adhesions between the pulmonary artery and the left upper lobe bronchus. The surgeon articulates the instrument to feel for resistance, ensuring safe dissection in this challenging area. A leader cath is used to pull the staple around the bronchus, followed by the insertion of a Purple Forty-Five stapler through the sub-xiphoid port.

The surgeon uses Johan wristed instruments to safely diathermy a small bridge left by a stapler near the pulmonary artery. The posterior segmental artery is identified with an adherent lymph node, which is maneuvered using Maryland articulated instruments to facilitate stapling. The posterior segmental vessel of the left upper lobe is stapled using a gold tip tan from a sub-xiphoid approach. The lingular artery is then identified, ensuring it is not mistaken for a segment six artery.

The surgeon uses articulated instruments to dissect lymph nodes from surrounding tissue, achieving precise articulation to navigate around a vessel. The instrument is angled to dissect tissue posteriorly without needing to change tools.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the final artery, bronchus, and vein before addressing station five and six lymph nodes. A benign-looking station five lymph node is removed, followed by a lower nodule on the pulmonary artery that appears positive. Using wristed instruments, the surgeon carefully lifts and angles the nodule, ensuring no damage to the pulmonary artery. Bipolar energy is applied to safely dissect the positive lymph node off the pulmonary artery, taking it as a single sample.

The surgeon removes the left upper lobe through the sub-xiphoid port, utilizing an eight-millimeter incision in the intercostal spaces and a three-centimeter sub-xiphoid port. An extra pleural catheter is inserted for long-term pain relief in high-risk patients.
